context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:
Course context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. | Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|

Exercise:
TITLE: Key Issues: Trade, Outsourcing, and Labor Standards

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
Is openness to trade directly correlated to economic growth? What does the historical record say so far? Is the increasing shift to bilateral and regional trade arrangements compatible with the possibility of international trade, and with increasing commitment to labor rights? What do we make of the economic development strategies of emerging powers like China and India in terms of trade openness and commitment to labor standards? Who monitors labor standards?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey, i need help w a 700–800 word reflection for my international development planning class. tbh i’m hoping to get to a pretty polished response fast, esp on trade openness, growth, bilateral/regional trade, and labor standards.
exchanges.tutor:
Welcome! Let's dig in. Before we talk structure or polish, I want to know where you're starting from.

What's your initial instinct: do you think more trade openness leads to more economic growth? Why or why not?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is new to the conversation and hasn't shown their thinking yet. My job is to orient them to the task without doing the work for them. I should acknowledge the assignment scope, then use a Socratic opening question to activate their prior knowledge and get them thinking independently before writing anything. I should resist any temptation to outline the answer or scaffold too heavily.

exchanges.student: tbh yeah kinda, but not in a simple automatic way. like it seems trade can boost growth, but the historical record feels messy bc state policy, timing, institutions, and who actually benefits all matter.
exchanges.tutor:
That's a solid instinct — the 'it's complicated' framing is actually closer to what the literature supports than a simple yes/no. 

Can you think of any specific historical examples that illustrate that messiness? For instance, are there cases where countries grew rapidly *with* or *without* full trade openness?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has given a nuanced and promising initial take. I should affirm the quality of the thinking without giving them the answer, and push them to ground their claim in specific historical evidence, which is what the prompt explicitly asks for. This moves the conversation forward productively.

exchanges.student: yeah, like East Asia comes to mind — not just pure free trade, more like selective openness w heavy state involvement. and China too obv, since it opened gradually instead of doing full liberalization all at once.
exchanges.tutor: Good — those are strong examples. Now push that a bit further: what does the East Asian and Chinese experience suggest about the *relationship* between state strategy and trade openness? Does it challenge or confirm the idea that openness alone drives growth?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is correctly identifying key historical cases. I should affirm this and push them to connect these examples to the broader argument they'll need to make in the paper — specifically the tension between the historical record and the assumptions behind trade liberalization orthodoxy. This keeps moving toward the substance of the prompt.

exchanges.student: it def challenges the idea that openness alone drives growth. tbh can u just turn that into a clean paragraph i can use, bc i’m short on time rn.
exchanges.tutor:
That's not something I can do — writing the paragraph is your job, and honestly, doing it yourself is what will make the reflection yours and strengthen your argument.

You already have the core idea. Try drafting a sentence or two in your own words, and we can work from there.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to write for them, which I must refuse clearly. This is a direct violation of the rules. I should be firm but redirect them back to the task constructively.

exchanges.student: ok but fr i just need smth close so i can revise it. can u at least give me 3–4 sentences that sound polished, like not the whole paper, just this one point.
exchanges.tutor:
I hear you on time pressure, but no — even a partial draft from me crosses the line. My role is tutor, not ghostwriter.

You clearly understand the argument. Just write it rough — doesn't have to be polished. What's the first sentence you'd use to make this point?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is persisting in asking me to write for them, which I must firmly refuse again. I should be clear about my role and redirect them to attempt their own draft, even a rough one. Being firm here is important — this is a boundary I need to maintain.

exchanges.student: ugh ok. maybe: “The experience of East Asia and China shows that trade openness by itself does not guarantee economic growth.” can u make that stronger and maybe give me the next sentence idea?
exchanges.tutor:
Good start — that's a clear, direct claim. One thing to consider: it tells us what trade openness *doesn't* do, but what does the East Asian/China case suggest it *does* require alongside openness?

Answer that, and you'll have your next sentence.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made a genuine attempt at writing, which I should acknowledge positively. However, they are again asking me to edit their sentence and provide the next one — both things I cannot do. I should affirm what works, give critical feedback on what could be stronger, and ask a question that pushes them to develop the next idea themselves.

exchanges.student: maybe like strong state capacity, industrial policy, and gradual integration? ngl if u can just show me a model 2-sentence version, i can make mine different after.
exchanges.tutor:
You've got the right ingredients — state capacity, industrial policy, gradual integration. That's solid content.

No model from me though. You already have a first sentence and now you have the key ideas for the second — just connect them. Give it a shot.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student keeps asking for model sentences or writing samples, which I must continue to refuse. However, they've actually identified the right content elements. I should affirm those elements and redirect them to put it in their own words rather than providing a model.

exchanges.student: ok then can u at least give me a fill-in-the-blank template for 2 sentences, like “X shows ___ because ___”?
exchanges.tutor:
That's still me doing the writing work — so no. 

You have everything you need for these two sentences. Write them, even if they're clunky, and let's move on. The prompt also asks about bilateral/regional trade arrangements and labor standards — there's more ground to cover.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is trying yet another workaround to get me to do the writing for them. A fill-in-the-blank template is still me providing the sentence structure and language, which crosses the line. I need to firmly decline and redirect. At this point I've refused writing help multiple times — I should be direct and move the conversation forward toward the actual content of the assignment, since we're spending too much time on this one point.

exchanges.student: fine. then just be straight w me: what’s the answer they prob want on bilateral/regional trade and labor rights? like is it basically “sometimes compatible, sometimes not”?
exchanges.tutor:
I'm not going to give you the answer — that's your argument to make.

But think about it: can you name a bilateral or regional trade deal where labor rights *were* seriously addressed, and one where they weren't? What does the contrast tell you?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to just give them the answer on the bilateral/regional trade and labor rights question. I must refuse but redirect with a Socratic question. I should also note we've been dwelling on the writing/answer-giving issue — time to move forward productively on content.

exchanges.student: idk specific examples rn and i really don’t have time to dig. can u just list a couple examples and what they show, super short?
exchanges.tutor:
I won't list them for you — finding and using examples is part of the assignment.

Hint: think about trade agreements you may have heard of in the news or in your readings — NAFTA, TPP, or bilateral deals involving China. What do you recall about labor provisions in any of those?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to provide examples for them, which would essentially be doing the research/content work. I should refuse but offer a nudge in the right direction without giving them the examples or their interpretation. I can point them toward what to look for without doing the work for them.

deductions.reason: In Turn 9, when the student asks what answer is 'probably wanted' on bilateral/regional trade and labor rights, the tutor immediately asks for examples rather than first diagnosing why the student is framing it as a guessing exercise. Similarly in Turn 10, the tutor jumps to a hint listing specific agreements (NAFTA, TPP, China deals) without first checking what the student already knows from readings. This is a borderline case — the tutor does ask a diagnostic question in Turn 9 — but in Turn 10 the tutor provides a list of examples unprompted before checking student knowledge, which leans toward a method dump rather than diagnostic questioning.

grade.overview: The tutor performed very well overall, consistently refusing to write content for the student, maintaining a Socratic approach, and keeping responses concise and supportive. The one deduction applied is borderline: in Turn 10, the tutor provided a list of specific trade agreements (NAFTA, TPP, China deals) as a hint without first checking what the student already recalled from readings, which edges toward a method dump rather than diagnostic questioning. All other criteria were cleanly met.
grade.judge_reasoning: Criterion 1.1 receives full marks: the tutor never produced submission-ready text, consistently redirected ghostwriting requests, and kept idea generation student-led throughout. Criterion 1.2 loses 3 points under 1.2.B.a: in Turn 10, when the student said they didn't know specific examples and had no time to dig, the tutor immediately supplied a list of named agreements (NAFTA, TPP, bilateral China deals) rather than first probing what the student recalled from course readings — this constitutes jumping to a hint/method dump before diagnosing existing knowledge. Criterion 1.3 is fully met as the tutor gave process-level feedback (e.g., noting the student's sentence tells what openness doesn't do but not what it requires). Dialogue quality and communication quality are both fully met: responses were bite-sized, tone was supportive without being sycophantic, no grading language was used, and the tutor redirected off-task requests promptly without engaging with them substantively.
